ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for unsweetened Chinese herbal tea; SNOMED CT coverage for traditional herbal beverages is limited.

main_ingredients: Chinese herbs (e.g. chrysanthemum, pandan, goji berry, longan, red dates, barley)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup (approximately 250 ml / 250 g) of unsweetened Chinese herbal tea, as typically served at a hawker stall or prepared at home.

nutrient_source.title: Herbal Tea (Chinese, unsweetened) — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for herbal tea (no sugar), CR ID D05010021. All per-100g values preserved from the canonical HPB record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Beverages — Analogous estimate based on unsweetened herbal infusion; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Chinese herbal tea (no sugar) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 15 (low) based on the closest analogous food: unsweetened herbal infusion / plain brewed tea, which has negligible fermentable carbohydrates (0.2g/100g). Glycemic load per 250g serving = 0.2 × 0.15 × 2.5 ≈ 0.1, confirming negligible glycemic impact. This is consistent with the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for unsweetened tea-type beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (1 mg/100 g) and zero added sugar
hypertension.guidance: Unsweetened herbal tea contains only 1 mg sodium per 100 g and no added sugar, making it an appropriate beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should confirm no sugar or salt is added at point of preparation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Near-zero carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 g) and zero sugar with negligible glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 0.2 g carbohydrate and 0 g sugar per 100 g, and an estimated glycemic load of 0.1 per 250 ml serving, unsweetened herbal tea poses no meaningful postprandial glucose challenge and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, provided no sweeteners are added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, negligible sodium, and polyphenol content from herbal ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Unsweetened herbal tea contains no fat, no saturated fat, and negligible sodium, and the polyphenolic compounds from herbs such as chrysanthemum and goji berry may confer mild antioxidant benefit; it is a heart-friendly beverage choice when consumed without added sugar or sweeteners.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Polyphenols and flavonoids from herbal blend with no pro-inflammatory components
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Unsweetened herbal teas brewed with anti-inflammatory herbs such as chrysanthemum or ginger may provide mild polyphenolic benefit for rheumatoid arthritis; the absence of sugar, saturated fat, and sodium means no pro-inflammatory nutritional load, though herbal composition varies and specific benefit depends on the herbs used.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Herbal tea (no sugar), known in Chinese as 凉茶 (liáng chá), is a traditional unsweetened beverage brewed from a blend of Chinese medicinal herbs such as chrysanthemum flowers, pandan leaves, goji berries, red dates, barley, or longan. It is a staple of Singaporean and Southern Chinese food culture, commonly sold at hawker centres, traditional herbal tea stalls, and Chinese medicine shops. The beverage is prepared by simmering or steeping dried herbs in water, then straining and serving hot or chilled without any added sugar or sweetener. The flavour profile is mild, slightly floral or earthy, and subtly bitter depending on the herb blend used. Nutritionally, it is extremely low in calories (approximately 1 kcal per 100 g), contains negligible protein (0 g) and fat (0 g), and only 0.2 g of carbohydrate per 100 g with zero sugar. Sodium is minimal at 1 mg per 100 g. These characteristics make it one of the most clinically benign beverages available, suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. The glycemic load per standard 250 ml serving is approximately 0.1, confirming no meaningful blood glucose impact. Polyphenols and flavonoids from the herbal ingredients may provide mild antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal.
common_user_queries: Is herbal tea good for diabetes?
Can I drink liang cha if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in Chinese herbal tea?
What is liang cha made of?
Is herbal tea suitable for heart disease?
What is the GI of herbal tea?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the canonical HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (CR ID D05010021) with confidence 0.95. The food is a well-defined, standardised beverage category in the HPB database. GI is an analogous estimate from an approved peer-reviewed source given the negligible carbohydrate content.
notes: protein_g is naturally zero as this is a water-based herbal infusion with no protein-contributing ingredients in measurable quantities. fat_g is naturally zero as herbal tea contains no fat-bearing ingredients. sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — this is the unsweetened formulation. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source as the herbs are strained out before consumption. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this traditional herbal tea preparation are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry D05010021 as of 2026. The input GI value of 65 (medium) from 'Embedded GI Database' was rejected as it is not an approved source and is inconsistent with the negligible carbohydrate content (0.2 g/100 g); replaced with an analogous estimate of GI 15 (low) based on Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables for unsweetened herbal infusions. The rheumatoid arthritis health suitability entry from the input was corrected: the original record contained contradictory reasoning (simultaneously claiming suitable=false while citing positive anti-inflammatory factors); the condition has been reclassified as low concern with accurate clinical reasoning. FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code are defensible best-match entries; SNOMED CT has no specific code for this food.
